0: Comparative analyses of pig farming management systems using the Life Cycle Assessment method

A R T|ABSTRACT Article history: Received 2 November 2018 Received in revised form 28 August 2019 Accepted 5 September 2019 Available online 6 September 2019 Handling Editor: CT Lee Keywords: Life cycle assessment Pig farming Manure management Environmental impact|The Life Cycle Assessment method was used to analyse and compare the environmental impacts of three systems of pig farming management: pig farming with pig manure fertilisation; pig farming using a manure treatment and a filtration method involving the production of mineral-organic fertilisers from manure; and pig farming in which 50% of the manure produced per y was used as fertiliser, and the other 50% was used in the treatment and filtration method. The LCA results revealed that fertiliser production from the manure processed by the treatment and filtration method had the greatest environmental impact. Direct fertilisation with manure was characterised by a 14.2% lesser impact, and combined fer- tiliser production from treated manure and direct manure fertilisation had a 7.0% lesser impact. The proper use of manure as a fertiliser could be the best option. The proposed system of pig manure management entails the simultaneous direct application of pig manure as fertiliser and its processing into mineral-organic fertiliser and could be an interesting environmental alternative. © 2019 Elsevier Ltd. 1: Pigs are among the most commonly raised animals in the world. They are the primary protein source for people across different geographical regions. As of 2018, there were approximately 772.5 M pigs worldwide. In 2018, China had the most pigs at 433 M head. The European Union (EU; 150 M head) and United States (73.2 M head) were second and third on this list. Germany, Spain, France, Denmark, the Netherlands and Poland bred for 70% of the EU pigs (Statista, 2019). Pig manure production of the 28 member countries of the EU (EU-28) amounts to 171.2 At/y. Germany, Spain and France are the biggest pig manure producers and account for over 19%, 18% and 9% of production (Fernández et al., 2015).

Pig manure is not only a waste but is also a valuable resource in terms of nutrients (e.g., N and P) for crops. The composition of pig manure depends on the type and age of the animals, their physical condition and the feeding method. The improper management of pig manure can harm the environment due to different types of emissions (e.g., NH3 and greenhouse gases) (Sommer et al., 2008; Prapaspongsa et al., 2010). Most of the existing plants that treat pig manure with energy recovery methods based on anaerobic digestion technology. Worldwide, the most common system is either a handling system without energy recovery or the direct application of the manure to the land. Manure management is a central topic in the agronomic and environmental analyses of intensive livestock production systems (Burton and Turner, 2003; Makara and Kowalski, 2016).

Pig manure management from its generation to its ultimate use on the land is necessary to achieve a sustainable direction in pig manure management. Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) aims to assess the overall environmental impacts considering all emissions, resource usages and production throughout a product's life cycle (ISO, 2006), whereas Material Flow Analysis (MFA) emphasises only the material flow and resources (Finnveden and Moberg, 2005).
* Corresponding author. E-mail address: amak@chemia.pk.edu.pl (A. Makara).
The Danish manure normative system (DMNS) is a simple input- output model for estimating the N and P excretions from pigs. The
DMNS has estimated the nutrient flows of N, and K in terms of the excretions and losses from pigs and systems at different stages of handling (Poulsen et al., 2006).

The environmental performance of a pig manure treatment system involving the centrifugation and subsequent biological removal of N from the liquid fraction and the composting of the solid fraction was assessed with LCA using the ReCiPe method (Corbala-Robles et al., 2018). Such treatment system typical in Flanders, Belgium was compared with the direct field application of raw manure. The comparison revealed that the treatment system produced better scores in some categories and vice versa. The environmental benefits from avoided fertiliser production made an important contribution to the LCA results (Hanserud et al., 2018).

Twelve scenarios involving the applications of various treat- ment, storage and land application systems were developed and compared using the LCA method (Prapaspongsa et al., 2010). The key for effective environmental impact reduction was found to be the integration of treatment technology systems with high nutrient recovery and strong control of greenhouse gas emissions at every stage of handling. Regarding global warming and aquatic eutro- phication effects, the anaerobic digestion-based scenario achieved the greatest impact reduction because of its high efficiencies in energy and nutrient recovery and restricted emissions of green- house gases. Regarding respiratory inorganics and terrestrial eutrophication effects, the incineration and thermal gasification- based scenarios and the scenario without a treatment system that applied the deep injection method yielded the greatest impact minimisation because it achieved the lowest NH3 emissions. This study indicates that all significant impacts need to be considered to decide upon the best management options.

Two scenarios of pig slurry management (Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2009) have been compared using the LCA method, i.e., the trans- fer of slurry and its injection into crop land, and the treatment of slurry in a collective biological treatment station. This study was based on a real case in the west of France. The two management strategies were similar with respect to climate change, but the eutrophication and acidification were twice as great for the treat- ment method relative to the transfer method. The overall envi- ronmental performance of the transfer method was better than that of the treatment method because it involved less acidification, eutrophication and non-renewable energy use.

LCA has been used to assess a technology that separates pig manure into solid and liquid fractions and de-waters the liquid fraction via reverse osmosis (De Vries et al., 2012). The resulting liquid mineral concentrate is then used as a mineral N and fer- tiliser, and the solid fraction is used for bio-energy production or as a P fertiliser. Scenarios involving this processing method and methods involving an additional anaerobic digestion of the solid fraction were compared to a reference system that only applied liquid manure. The scenarios involving only manure processing resulted in increased environmental impacts among most of the compared impact categories. While marine eutrophication did not change, terrestrial acidification and particulate matter formation increased by up to 44% due to NH3 and NOx emissions from the processing and storage of the solid fractions. Authors concluded that processing pig manure increased the overall environmental consequences compared to direct use of raw manure as a fertiliser. LCA has been applied (Hoeve et al., 2014) to compare the ap- plications of raw pig slurry to arable land and using four treatment technologies: separation by a mechanical screw press, screw press separation with composting of the solid fraction, separation with a decanter centrifuge, and decanter centrifuge separation with NH3 stripping of the liquid fraction. In all scenarios, the liquid fraction was applied as a fertiliser on a pig farm, and the solid fraction was transported and utilised for crop production. Separation, especially
via centrifuge, was found to result in a lower environmental impact potential than the application of raw manure to the arable land.

For model systems of manure management on farm land and centralised strategies (Flotats et al., 2009), the problems include the cost of manure transport regardless of the size of the farm. Phase separation can be used to enhance manure management capability. The solid phase can be transported further distances, and the liquid fraction can be used on nearby land via irrigation systems, or it can be further processed (Burton, 2007).

Makara and Kowalski (2018) used the Best Available Techniques Not Entailing Excessive Costs (BATNEEC) method to evaluate three selected systems of pig manure management. The system proposed for pig manure management in this study was as follows: elimi- nation of manure storage in lagoons, application of roughly half of the pig manure as fertiliser, processing of the other half via the AMAK method on each farm, and use of a separated solid phase to produce mineral-organic fertilisers in one central unit.

The present work used Polish pig farms as the study subjects. LCA evaluations were performed using 16 impact categories. The application of LCA to farm-scale systems allowed for the compari- son of the environmental impacts of different pig farming models. The following three different scenarios were developed, and their environmental performances were compared: I - pig farming with fertilisation using pig manure; II - pig farming with the AMAK pig manure processing method, which involves the production of mineral-organic fertilisers from manure (Makara and Kowalski, 2015, 2016); and III - pig farming in which 50% of the pig manure produced per y was used directly as a fertiliser, and the other 50% was subjected to the AMAK method of mineral-organic fertiliser production from manure (i.e., the combined method). This study provides a theoretical reference for the reduction of the environ- mental impact of pig farms.

The Danish manure normative system (DMNS) is a simple input- output model for estimating the N and P excretions from pigs. The
DMNS has estimated the nutrient flows of N, and K in terms of the excretions and losses from pigs and systems at different stages of handling (Poulsen et al., 2006).

The environmental performance of a pig manure treatment system involving the centrifugation and subsequent biological removal of N from the liquid fraction and the composting of the solid fraction was assessed with LCA using the ReCiPe method (Corbala-Robles et al., 2018). Such treatment system typical in Flanders, Belgium was compared with the direct field application of raw manure. The comparison revealed that the treatment system produced better scores in some categories and vice versa. The environmental benefits from avoided fertiliser production made an important contribution to the LCA results (Hanserud et al., 2018).

Twelve scenarios involving the applications of various treat- ment, storage and land application systems were developed and compared using the LCA method (Prapaspongsa et al., 2010). The key for effective environmental impact reduction was found to be the integration of treatment technology systems with high nutrient recovery and strong control of greenhouse gas emissions at every stage of handling. Regarding global warming and aquatic eutro- phication effects, the anaerobic digestion-based scenario achieved the greatest impact reduction because of its high efficiencies in energy and nutrient recovery and restricted emissions of green- house gases. Regarding respiratory inorganics and terrestrial eutrophication effects, the incineration and thermal gasification- based scenarios and the scenario without a treatment system that applied the deep injection method yielded the greatest impact minimisation because it achieved the lowest NH3 emissions. This study indicates that all significant impacts need to be considered to decide upon the best management options.

Two scenarios of pig slurry management (Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2009) have been compared using the LCA method, i.e., the trans- fer of slurry and its injection into crop land, and the treatment of slurry in a collective biological treatment station. This study was based on a real case in the west of France. The two management strategies were similar with respect to climate change, but the eutrophication and acidification were twice as great for the treat- ment method relative to the transfer method. The overall envi- ronmental performance of the transfer method was better than that of the treatment method because it involved less acidification, eutrophication and non-renewable energy use.

LCA has been used to assess a technology that separates pig manure into solid and liquid fractions and de-waters the liquid fraction via reverse osmosis (De Vries et al., 2012). The resulting liquid mineral concentrate is then used as a mineral N and fer- tiliser, and the solid fraction is used for bio-energy production or as a P fertiliser. Scenarios involving this processing method and methods involving an additional anaerobic digestion of the solid fraction were compared to a reference system that only applied liquid manure. The scenarios involving only manure processing resulted in increased environmental impacts among most of the compared impact categories. While marine eutrophication did not change, terrestrial acidification and particulate matter formation increased by up to 44% due to NH3 and NOx emissions from the processing and storage of the solid fractions. Authors concluded that processing pig manure increased the overall environmental consequences compared to direct use of raw manure as a fertiliser. LCA has been applied (Hoeve et al., 2014) to compare the ap- plications of raw pig slurry to arable land and using four treatment technologies: separation by a mechanical screw press, screw press separation with composting of the solid fraction, separation with a decanter centrifuge, and decanter centrifuge separation with NH3 stripping of the liquid fraction. In all scenarios, the liquid fraction was applied as a fertiliser on a pig farm, and the solid fraction was transported and utilised for crop production. Separation, especially
via centrifuge, was found to result in a lower environmental impact potential than the application of raw manure to the arable land.

For model systems of manure management on farm land and centralised strategies (Flotats et al., 2009), the problems include the cost of manure transport regardless of the size of the farm. Phase separation can be used to enhance manure management capability. The solid phase can be transported further distances, and the liquid fraction can be used on nearby land via irrigation systems, or it can be further processed (Burton, 2007).

Makara and Kowalski (2018) used the Best Available Techniques Not Entailing Excessive Costs (BATNEEC) method to evaluate three selected systems of pig manure management. The system proposed for pig manure management in this study was as follows: elimi- nation of manure storage in lagoons, application of roughly half of the pig manure as fertiliser, processing of the other half via the AMAK method on each farm, and use of a separated solid phase to produce mineral-organic fertilisers in one central unit.

Germany, Spain, France, Denmark, the Netherlands and Poland bred for 70% of the EU pigs (Statista, 2019). Pig manure production of the 28 member countries of the EU (EU-28) amounts to 171.2 At/y. Germany, Spain and France are the biggest pig manure producers and account for over 19%, 18% and 9% of production (Fernández et al., 2015).

Pig manure is not only a waste but is also a valuable resource in terms of nutrients (e.g., N and P) for crops. The composition of pig manure depends on the type and age of the animals, their physical condition and the feeding method. The improper management of pig manure can harm the environment due to different types of emissions (e.g., NH3 and greenhouse gases) (Sommer et al., 2008; Prapaspongsa et al., 2010). Most of the existing plants that treat pig manure with energy recovery methods based on anaerobic digestion technology. Worldwide, the most common system is either a handling system without energy recovery or the direct application of the manure to the land. Manure management is a central topic in the agronomic and environmental analyses of intensive livestock production systems (Burton and Turner, 2003; Makara and Kowalski, 2016).

Pig manure management from its generation to its ultimate use on the land is necessary to achieve a sustainable direction in pig manure management. Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) aims to assess the overall environmental impacts considering all emissions, resource usages and production throughout a product's life cycle (ISO, 2006), whereas Material Flow Analysis (MFA) emphasises only the material flow and resources (Finnveden and Moberg, 2005).

The Danish manure normative system (DMNS) is a simple input- output model for estimating the N and P excretions from pigs. The
DMNS has estimated the nutrient flows of N, and K in terms of the excretions and losses from pigs and systems at different stages of handling (Poulsen et al., 2006).

The environmental performance of a pig manure treatment system involving the centrifugation and subsequent biological removal of N from the liquid fraction and the composting of the solid fraction was assessed with LCA using the ReCiPe method (Corbala-Robles et al., 2018). Such treatment system typical in Flanders, Belgium was compared with the direct field application of raw manure. The comparison revealed that the treatment system produced better scores in some categories and vice versa. The environmental benefits from avoided fertiliser production made an important contribution to the LCA results (Hanserud et al., 2018).

Twelve scenarios involving the applications of various treat- ment, storage and land application systems were developed and compared using the LCA method (Prapaspongsa et al., 2010). The key for effective environmental impact reduction was found to be the integration of treatment technology systems with high nutrient recovery and strong control of greenhouse gas emissions at every stage of handling. Regarding global warming and aquatic eutro- phication effects, the anaerobic digestion-based scenario achieved the greatest impact reduction because of its high efficiencies in energy and nutrient recovery and restricted emissions of green- house gases. Regarding respiratory inorganics and terrestrial eutrophication effects, the incineration and thermal gasification- based scenarios and the scenario without a treatment system that applied the deep injection method yielded the greatest impact minimisation because it achieved the lowest NH3 emissions. This study indicates that all significant impacts need to be considered to decide upon the best management options.

Two scenarios of pig slurry management (Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2009) have been compared using the LCA method, i.e., the trans- fer of slurry and its injection into crop land, and the treatment of slurry in a collective biological treatment station. This study was based on a real case in the west of France. The two management strategies were similar with respect to climate change, but the eutrophication and acidification were twice as great for the treat- ment method relative to the transfer method. The overall envi- ronmental performance of the transfer method was better than that of the treatment method because it involved less acidification, eutrophication and non-renewable energy use.

LCA has been used to assess a technology that separates pig manure into solid and liquid fractions and de-waters the liquid fraction via reverse osmosis (De Vries et al., 2012). The resulting liquid mineral concentrate is then used as a mineral N and fer- tiliser, and the solid fraction is used for bio-energy production or as a P fertiliser. Scenarios involving this processing method and methods involving an additional anaerobic digestion of the solid fraction were compared to a reference system that only applied liquid manure. The scenarios involving only manure processing resulted in increased environmental impacts among most of the compared impact categories. While marine eutrophication did not change, terrestrial acidification and particulate matter formation increased by up to 44% due to NH3 and NOx emissions from the processing and storage of the solid fractions. Authors concluded that processing pig manure increased the overall environmental consequences compared to direct use of raw manure as a fertiliser. LCA has been applied (Hoeve et al., 2014) to compare the ap- plications of raw pig slurry to arable land and using four treatment technologies: separation by a mechanical screw press, screw press separation with composting of the solid fraction, separation with a decanter centrifuge, and decanter centrifuge separation with NH3 stripping of the liquid fraction. In all scenarios, the liquid fraction was applied as a fertiliser on a pig farm, and the solid fraction was transported and utilised for crop production. Separation, especially
via centrifuge, was found to result in a lower environmental impact potential than the application of raw manure to the arable land.

For model systems of manure management on farm land and centralised strategies (Flotats et al., 2009), the problems include the cost of manure transport regardless of the size of the farm. Phase separation can be used to enhance manure management capability. The solid phase can be transported further distances, and the liquid fraction can be used on nearby land via irrigation systems, or it can be further processed (Burton, 2007).

Makara and Kowalski (2018) used the Best Available Techniques Not Entailing Excessive Costs (BATNEEC) method to evaluate three selected systems of pig manure management. The system proposed for pig manure management in this study was as follows: elimi- nation of manure storage in lagoons, application of roughly half of the pig manure as fertiliser, processing of the other half via the AMAK method on each farm, and use of a separated solid phase to produce mineral-organic fertilisers in one central unit.

The present work used Polish pig farms as the study subjects. LCA evaluations were performed using 16 impact categories. The application of LCA to farm-scale systems allowed for the compari- son of the environmental impacts of different pig farming models. The following three different scenarios were developed, and their environmental performances were compared: I - pig farming with fertilisation using pig manure; II - pig farming with the AMAK pig manure processing method, which involves the production of mineral-organic fertilisers from manure (Makara and Kowalski, 2015, 2016); and III - pig farming in which 50% of the pig manure produced per y was used directly as a fertiliser, and the other 50% was subjected to the AMAK method of mineral-organic fertiliser production from manure (i.e., the combined method). This study provides a theoretical reference for the reduction of the environ- mental impact of pig farms.
10: 2.1. Goal and scope of the study

The LCA evaluations were performed using the ISO standards (ISO, 2006) and the International Reference Life Cycle Data System (ILCD) 2011 Midpoint + V.1.09 method (European Commission, 2012; Goedkoop et al., 2009), which proposes a combined midpoint/endpoint approach with 16 impact categories. This method supports the correct use of the characterisation factors for impact assessment as recommended in the ILCD guidance docu- ment (European Commission, 2011).

The ILCD is representative for European conditions, and the final result of the analysis is an "eco-indicator" that provides a value for the impact on the environment. The normalisation coefficients used for the analysis were assumed based on the Product Envi- ronmental Footprint (PEF) guide (European Commission, 2012a). The ILCD weight coefficients were not used in accordance with the recommendations of the PEF guide, and the weight indicator was assumed to be equal to 1. This approach means that all categories of environmental impact were treated equally. The calculations were performed with SimaPro LCA software (SimaPro, 2016).

The functional unit was assumed to be 1 kg of carcass weight. One hundred kilograms of live weight was assumed to equal 80 kg of carcass weight or 75 kg of pork (Reckmann et al., 2016). The scope of the study covered all processes connected with pig breeding and pig manure utilisation (i.e., from cradle to grave).

13: Scenario I involved pig farming with the use of 248,878 t of all pig manure produced in 1 y as fertiliser, during a 6-month period (Stoklosa, 2015; Makara and Kowalski, 2016). This resulted in an average storage time for the liquid manure in lagoons of approxi- mately 6 months as given in (Regulation EC, 2003).

The average dose of applied manure was 44 t/ha, and the total N application rate was 86 kg/ha. The average dose of P2O5 was 23 kg/ ha, and that of K2O was 51 kg/ha. The average dose of pig manure used in Polish agriculture is 50 t/ha (Czuba, 1996; Makara and Kowalski, 2018).

A calculation of manure transport costs (Kowalski, 2017) indi- cated that manure application for fertilisation was profitable at a maximum transport distance of 52 km. In the analysed case, the distances from the pig farms to the fields that could be fertilised with manure were in the range of 50-120 km.

Pig manure collected in housing systems has to be stored until it can be transported and spread on fields (Hansen, 2004). In Scenario I, over the course of 1 y, 118,829 of pig manure was stored for a minimum of 6 months in 5 open lagoons made from concrete. The volumes of the lagoons ranged from 20,000 to 100,000 m³ (Stoktosa, 2015).

N losses during the storage of the liquid livestock manure were strongly correlated with the storage time. Calculated based on the ash contents, the losses ranged between 1.4 and 20.5% of the total N content after 35 d and between 17.1 and 53.6% after 180 d. The differences depended on the origins of the slurries and also seemed to depend on the pH values (Dewes et al., 1990).

The study lagoon of the AgriFarm Company has a surface area of 5,050 m² and stored 15,000 t/y of manure. Calculations indicated that 1t of manure occupied 0.3 m² of lagoon surface area, and 240,000 t/y pig manure occupied 80,000 m² of land surface area. Odour emissions resulted in an average Specific Odour Emission Rate (SOER) of 2,280 SOER (ouE/S m²). A value of 1 our/m³ is equivalent to 40 ppb/m³ in-butanol (31 ppb/kg in air with a density d = 1.293 g/L) (Makara et al., 2016).

Scenario II employed pig farming with AMAK pig manure filtration method to produce mineral-organic fertiliser (350 kg/t of raw pig manure) from the separated solid phase and other fertiliser components. The amount of the produced separated solid phase (212 kg/t of raw pig manure) was 1/5 of the amount of the raw manure, and the amount of mineral-organic fertiliser produced was 1/3 of the amount of the raw manure. The amount of the produced filtrate was 626 kg/t of raw pig manure. Phase separation with the

AMAK method resulted in a 95% reduction in the chemical oxygen demand (COD) including an 80% reduction in N and a 99% reduction in P in the filtrate compared to their contents in the raw manure. The AMAK method also allowed for the elimination of odour emissions from the filtrate and the separated solid phase (Makara and Kowalski, 2015, 2016; Makara, 2016).

The separated solid phase was used to produce mineral-organic fertiliser (Makara and Kowalski, 2016, 2018). The typical universal fertiliser applied to the soil had low P and microelement contents (N:P2O5:K2O (4:7.5:7.5) + 0.1 B, 0.1 Cu, 0.2 Mn, 0.01 Mo and 0.2 Zn) and contained (per 1 t of fertiliser) 604.4 kg of the separated solid phase from the AMAK process and 395.6 kg of added fertiliser components. These components comprised macronutrients, i.e., (NH4)2SO4, KCl and MgCO3, and micronutrients, i.e., CuSO4 5H2O, MnSO4 H2O, Na2B4O7 10H20, ZnSO4 7H2O and (NH4)6M07O24 7H20. The consumption values for the raw materials used in the AMAK method and in the production of fertiliser employed in Scenarios II and III are presented in Table 5.

19: Table 1 details the breeding of pigs in Poland and the quantities of pig manure produced. The domestic production of slaughtered livestock amounted to 2,669,000 t in 2014 (Central Statistical Office, 2015; Pasternak and Calik, 2015). The factor for converting the number of breeding pigs into the total quantity of livestock in Poland was 4.39 head/t.

Manure, which is a mixture of faeces, urine and food and water remnants, is classified as a liquid organic fertiliser that can be used to fertilise arable land and grassland. Pig manure containing <8% dry matter is classified as diluted, and manure with a dry matter content >8% is classified as thick (Sommer et al., 2008; Makara and Kowalski, 2016). The content of minerals in the slurry depends on the dry matter content of the manure. The density of the manure was 1 t/m³.

The average composition of the pig manure from five AgriFarm Company farms was as follows (kg/t of liquid manure): Ntotal = 18.0; Nmineral = 11.0; P2O5 = and dry mass = 76.0 (Makara and Kowalski, 2018).
20: The manure originated from five AgriFarm Company pig farms in western Pomerania in Poland. The AgriFarm Company is also the owner of 30,000 ha of arable land of which only approximately 5,500 ha could be fertilised with manure. Both the number of pigs and the breeding proportion profile changed greatly in the period of 2008-2010 primarily because of the variation in the market demand for pork (Kowalski et al., 2013; Makara and Kowalski, 2016). The mean values of the pig manure produced per y over this period are presented in Table 2.

The five pig farms bred 97,901 head/y (Makara and Kowalski, 2018). The pig manure production was 240,878 t/y (average 46t/head). Applied conversion factor to convert the number of breeding pigs into the total quantity of livestock in Poland (4.39 head/t; see section 2.3), allowed to estimate the amount of livestock produced as 22,301 t/y or 17,841 t/y of produced carcass weight. The 2.46 of pig manure produced per head equalled 10.80 t/t of livestock and 13.50 t/t of carcass weight.

The standard rate of fertiliser manure application was 44t/ha (4.4 kg/m2), which resulted in a land use intensity for manure spreading of 3.07 /kg of carcass weight. The electricity con- sumption and odour emission level were estimated according to data collected by the AgriFarm Company (Kowalski, 2017). The estimated consumption of fuel with an average calorific value of 43 MJ/kg and density of 0.83 kg/L was 25 per 100 km. The trans- portation distance for the manure using a 12-m³ cistern was esti- mated to be 100 km. The consumption of energy for fertiliser spreading using a 12-m³ water car was 460 MJ/ha (15 MJ/t) based on (Zebrowska and Marczuk, 2015).

implemented their own large animal feed factory, which has since been used to produce a significant part of their own crops for an- imal feed production (Kowalski, 2017). The feed preparation data are presented in Table 3, and the housing system data (for a fully slatted floor) are presented in Table 4.

The Agri-footprint database (Blonk, 2015) was used for the following processes: cultivation, processing and feed production, and pig breeding. Additionally, the European Life Cycle Database (ELCD) was used for the process of drinking water production, and electricity and heat were calculated based on the Polish system (Lelek et al., 2016). These data have been modified with real data from Polish farms. The data used for the modelling of the liquid manure management for the analysed scenarios came from the five above-mentioned Polish pig farms (Kowalski, 2017). In the case of Scenario II, the data were based on development research on the innovative AMAK technology (Makara and Kowalski, 2016). Balance sheet data for the comparative analyses of the three scenarios are provided in Table 5.

When manure was used for fertilisation, the avoided environ- mental burden approach was used to account for the environ- mental benefits of substituting manure for the reference fertiliser. Scenario I involved the use of all manure produced over 1 y as fertiliser during a 6-month period (Kowalski, 2017). This process resulted in an average liquid manure storage time in lagoons of approximately 6 months as given in (Regulation EC, 2003). To prepare an inventory table for the primary production, existing data in the Agri-footprint datasets were used and included a mixture of (NH4)2SO4 (NPK 21-0-0), double superphosphate (NPK 0-48-0) and KCI (NPK 0-0-60). One t of pig manure provided

12.33 kg of the reference fertiliser. To obtain 12.33 kg of fertiliser from the raw materials (NPK 7.5-0.9-16), 9.31 kg (NH4)2SO4 1.09 kg double superphosphate and 1.93 kg KCI were included in the mix.

In Scenario II, i.e., the AMAK process, 350 kg of NPK fertiliser (4- 7.5-7.5) was produced per 1 t of pig manure. To prepare an in- ventory table for the primary production, existing data in the Agri- footprint datasets were used and included a mixture of (NH4)2 SO4 (NPK 21-0-0), double superphosphate (NPK 0-48-0) and KCI (NPK 0-0-60). To obtain 350 kg of fertiliser (NPK 4-7.5-7.5) from the raw materials, 141 kg (NH4)2SO4, 116 kg double superphosphate and 93 kg KCI were mixed.

In Scenario III, 50% of the pig manure produced each y was used as fertiliser on the farm's own fields over a period of 6 months, and the other 50% was processed during the second 6 months with AMAK pig manure treatment and the filtration method (i.e., the combined method), which involved the production of mineral- organic fertilisers from the manure.
Scenarios II and III completely eliminated the storage of pig manure in lagoons.

implemented their own large animal feed factory, which has since been used to produce a significant part of their own crops for an- imal feed production (Kowalski, 2017). The feed preparation data are presented in Table 3, and the housing system data (for a fully slatted floor) are presented in Table 4.

The Agri-footprint database (Blonk, 2015) was used for the following processes: cultivation, processing and feed production, and pig breeding. Additionally, the European Life Cycle Database (ELCD) was used for the process of drinking water production, and electricity and heat were calculated based on the Polish system (Lelek et al., 2016). These data have been modified with real data from Polish farms. The data used for the modelling of the liquid manure management for the analysed scenarios came from the five above-mentioned Polish pig farms (Kowalski, 2017). In the case of Scenario II, the data were based on development research on the innovative AMAK technology (Makara and Kowalski, 2016). Balance sheet data for the comparative analyses of the three scenarios are provided in Table 5.

When manure was used for fertilisation, the avoided environ- mental burden approach was used to account for the environ- mental benefits of substituting manure for the reference fertiliser. Scenario I involved the use of all manure produced over 1 y as fertiliser during a 6-month period (Kowalski, 2017). This process resulted in an average liquid manure storage time in lagoons of approximately 6 months as given in (Regulation EC, 2003). To prepare an inventory table for the primary production, existing data in the Agri-footprint datasets were used and included a mixture of (NH4)2SO4 (NPK 21-0-0), double superphosphate (NPK 0-48-0) and KCI (NPK 0-0-60). One t of pig manure provided

12.33 kg of the reference fertiliser. To obtain 12.33 kg of fertiliser from the raw materials (NPK 7.5-0.9-16), 9.31 kg (NH4)2SO4 1.09 kg double superphosphate and 1.93 kg KCI were included in the mix.

In Scenario II, i.e., the AMAK process, 350 kg of NPK fertiliser (4- 7.5-7.5) was produced per 1 t of pig manure. To prepare an in- ventory table for the primary production, existing data in the Agri- footprint datasets were used and included a mixture of (NH4)2 SO4 (NPK 21-0-0), double superphosphate (NPK 0-48-0) and KCI (NPK 0-0-60). To obtain 350 kg of fertiliser (NPK 4-7.5-7.5) from the raw materials, 141 kg (NH4)2SO4, 116 kg double superphosphate and 93 kg KCI were mixed.

In Scenario III, 50% of the pig manure produced each y was used as fertiliser on the farm's own fields over a period of 6 months, and the other 50% was processed during the second 6 months with AMAK pig manure treatment and the filtration method (i.e., the combined method), which involved the production of mineral- organic fertilisers from the manure.
Scenarios II and III completely eliminated the storage of pig manure in lagoons.

Scenario I resulted in the greatest impacts in four categories, i.e., AD, TE, ME and LO, and Scenario III produced the greatest impacts in the WRD and FD categories. The differences (in %) in each impact category between the scenarios are presented in Fig. 2.

The normalisation results (Fig. 3 and Table 7) revealed that, in terms of quality, the structures of the impacts upon the environ- ment appeared similar and were dominated by the HT NCE impact category. The categories with the next three highest scores were AD, TE and FE. The total impacts of these four dominant impact categories constituted more than 72% of the total impact in all scenarios. HT NCE was responsible for 35% of this impact in Sce- nario I, 44% in Scenario II and 40% in Scenario III.

The final results indicated that Scenario II, i.e., the AMAK fer- tiliser production from manure, exerted the greatest impact on the environment. Fertilisation with manure (Scenario I) was charac- terised by a 14.2% lesser impact, and combined AMAK production and direct manure fertilisation was characterised by a 7.0% lesser impact (Scenario III).
Based on the characterisation results, the housing system and
the pig feed/fattening process (i.e., the basic processes for all sce- narios) was are responsible for the highest impact in all scenarios (Figs. 4-6).
The housing system and the pig feed/fattening processes were responsible for 77% of the overall impact on the environment in Scenario I, 66% in Scenario II and 71% in Scenario III (Table 8).

In Scenario I, the fertilisation and storage of manure contributed 24% to the overall indicator. The avoided burden corresponding to the amount of manure produced and used for fertilisation contributed -20% to the AD impact category indicator and -1% to the overall impact. In Scenario II, the AMAK production made the greatest contribution to the total environmental impact indicator at 64%, but the avoided burden reached 29% of the total environ- mental impact in this scenario. In Scenario III, fertilisation with manure and the AMAK process contributed 46% of the overall burden. The environmental benefit accounted for -16% of this burden.

Scenario I resulted in the greatest impacts in four categories, i.e., AD, TE, ME and LO, and Scenario III produced the greatest impacts in the WRD and FD categories. The differences (in %) in each impact category between the scenarios are presented in Fig. 2.

The normalisation results (Fig. 3 and Table 7) revealed that, in terms of quality, the structures of the impacts upon the environ- ment appeared similar and were dominated by the HT NCE impact category. The categories with the next three highest scores were AD, TE and FE. The total impacts of these four dominant impact categories constituted more than 72% of the total impact in all scenarios. HT NCE was responsible for 35% of this impact in Sce- nario I, 44% in Scenario II and 40% in Scenario III.

The final results indicated that Scenario II, i.e., the AMAK fer- tiliser production from manure, exerted the greatest impact on the environment. Fertilisation with manure (Scenario I) was charac- terised by a 14.2% lesser impact, and combined AMAK production and direct manure fertilisation was characterised by a 7.0% lesser impact (Scenario III).
Based on the characterisation results, the housing system and
the pig feed/fattening process (i.e., the basic processes for all sce- narios) was are responsible for the highest impact in all scenarios (Figs. 4-6).
The housing system and the pig feed/fattening processes were responsible for 77% of the overall impact on the environment in Scenario I, 66% in Scenario II and 71% in Scenario III (Table 8).

In Scenario I, the fertilisation and storage of manure contributed 24% to the overall indicator. The avoided burden corresponding to the amount of manure produced and used for fertilisation contributed -20% to the AD impact category indicator and -1% to the overall impact. In Scenario II, the AMAK production made the greatest contribution to the total environmental impact indicator at 64%, but the avoided burden reached 29% of the total environ- mental impact in this scenario. In Scenario III, fertilisation with manure and the AMAK process contributed 46% of the overall burden. The environmental benefit accounted for -16% of this burden.

Scenario I resulted in the greatest impacts in four categories, i.e., AD, TE, ME and LO, and Scenario III produced the greatest impacts in the WRD and FD categories. The differences (in %) in each impact category between the scenarios are presented in Fig. 2.

The normalisation results (Fig. 3 and Table 7) revealed that, in terms of quality, the structures of the impacts upon the environ- ment appeared similar and were dominated by the HT NCE impact category. The categories with the next three highest scores were AD, TE and FE. The total impacts of these four dominant impact categories constituted more than 72% of the total impact in all scenarios. HT NCE was responsible for 35% of this impact in Sce- nario I, 44% in Scenario II and 40% in Scenario III.

The final results indicated that Scenario II, i.e., the AMAK fer- tiliser production from manure, exerted the greatest impact on the environment. Fertilisation with manure (Scenario I) was charac- terised by a 14.2% lesser impact, and combined AMAK production and direct manure fertilisation was characterised by a 7.0% lesser impact (Scenario III).
Based on the characterisation results, the housing system and
the pig feed/fattening process (i.e., the basic processes for all sce- narios) was are responsible for the highest impact in all scenarios (Figs. 4-6).
The housing system and the pig feed/fattening processes were responsible for 77% of the overall impact on the environment in Scenario I, 66% in Scenario II and 71% in Scenario III (Table 8).

In Scenario I, the fertilisation and storage of manure contributed 24% to the overall indicator. The avoided burden corresponding to the amount of manure produced and used for fertilisation contributed -20% to the AD impact category indicator and -1% to the overall impact. In Scenario II, the AMAK production made the greatest contribution to the total environmental impact indicator at 64%, but the avoided burden reached 29% of the total environ- mental impact in this scenario. In Scenario III, fertilisation with manure and the AMAK process contributed 46% of the overall burden. The environmental benefit accounted for -16% of this burden.

Scenario I resulted in the greatest impacts in four categories, i.e., AD, TE, ME and LO, and Scenario III produced the greatest impacts in the WRD and FD categories. The differences (in %) in each impact category between the scenarios are presented in Fig. 2.

The normalisation results (Fig. 3 and Table 7) revealed that, in terms of quality, the structures of the impacts upon the environ- ment appeared similar and were dominated by the HT NCE impact category. The categories with the next three highest scores were AD, TE and FE. The total impacts of these four dominant impact categories constituted more than 72% of the total impact in all scenarios. HT NCE was responsible for 35% of this impact in Sce- nario I, 44% in Scenario II and 40% in Scenario III.

The final results indicated that Scenario II, i.e., the AMAK fer- tiliser production from manure, exerted the greatest impact on the environment. Fertilisation with manure (Scenario I) was charac- terised by a 14.2% lesser impact, and combined AMAK production and direct manure fertilisation was characterised by a 7.0% lesser impact (Scenario III).
Based on the characterisation results, the housing system and
the pig feed/fattening process (i.e., the basic processes for all sce- narios) was are responsible for the highest impact in all scenarios (Figs. 4-6).
The housing system and the pig feed/fattening processes were responsible for 77% of the overall impact on the environment in Scenario I, 66% in Scenario II and 71% in Scenario III (Table 8).

In Scenario I, the fertilisation and storage of manure contributed 24% to the overall indicator. The avoided burden corresponding to the amount of manure produced and used for fertilisation contributed -20% to the AD impact category indicator and -1% to the overall impact. In Scenario II, the AMAK production made the greatest contribution to the total environmental impact indicator at 64%, but the avoided burden reached 29% of the total environ- mental impact in this scenario. In Scenario III, fertilisation with manure and the AMAK process contributed 46% of the overall burden. The environmental benefit accounted for -16% of this burden.

involves the production of mineral-organic fertilisers from manure and III - combined method of pig farming in which 50% of the produced pig manure was used directly as a fertiliser, and the other 50% was subjected to the AMAK method to produce mineral- organic fertilisers.

The key processes (basic for all scenarios) were identified as the housing system and the pig feed/fattening process and were responsible for 77% of the overall environmental impact in Scenario I, 66% in Scenario II and 71% in Scenario III.

LCA results indicated that Scenario II, resulted in the greatest impact on the environment. Scenario I was characterised by a 14.2% lesser impact, and Scenario III was characterised by a 7.0% lesser impact. The proper use of manure as a fertiliser can be environ- mentally beneficial. Unfortunately, the areas of farmland that can be fertilised with pig manure are continuously decreasing in Poland and Europe, and the management of this waste is becoming increasingly problematic. Scenarios II and III eliminated the storage of pig manure in lagoons.

Scenario III was proposed for pig manure management. The AMAK method allows for the continuous processing of manure originating from pig farming. During the periods when fertilisation is permissible, pig manure can also be used to directly fertilise fields.
